summary:
The Thinker of Hamangia is a celebrated prehistoric clay figurine discovered in 1956 at the site of Cernavoda in Dobruja (present-day southeastern Romania), dated to approximately 5000–4500 BCE and associated with the [[event:1563|Hamangia culture]]. The figurine depicts a seated human figure with hands resting on knees, head inclined forward in a posture of contemplation or meditation — an attitude so evocative of introspective thought that it was immediately named after Rodin's famous sculpture The Thinker and has become one of the most iconic prehistoric artworks in the world.

The Thinker was found together with a seated female figure, and the pair are thought to represent complementary aspects of prehistoric symbolism — perhaps a male-female couple, ancestors, or ritual figures with cosmological significance. Both figurines exhibit a refined naturalism unusual for their period and region, with carefully modeled limbs, stylized but expressive facial features, and a sense of psychological weight quite distinct from the more schematic figurines typical of European Neolithic traditions. The figures were found in a grave context, suggesting they accompanied the dead as ritual objects.

The Thinker of Hamangia is now held in the National History Museum of Romania in Bucharest and is considered a masterpiece of prehistoric art. It demonstrates that communities of the Neolithic and early Chalcolithic possessed the artistic sensitivity and symbolic vocabulary to create objects of enduring aesthetic and philosophical resonance, thousands of years before the development of writing or the literate philosophical traditions that are usually associated with such contemplative subjects.
